content.text: is the process of manually reconciling changes when two branches modify the same part of a file differently, causing Git to flag a
because it can't automatically decide which version to keep. In modern software engineering workflows, this happens within a structured pipeline: you create a
before your changes enter the main codebase.

Merge conflicts are inevitable in collaborative development. Without proper resolution:
- Code integration stalls, blocking team progress
- Incorrect changes might be merged, introducing bugs
- Team coordination breaks down when multiple developers edit the same files
Prevents broken builds and regression bugs
Maintains clear commit history for future debugging
Code review catches issues before merge
Resolution can take 15–60+ minutes for complex conflicts
AI can suggest but not fully auto-resolve complex conflicts
Wrong resolution choice can break functionality

main (stable)
  └── feature-branch (your work)
        └── commits A, B, C
receives new commits while you're working, both branches diverge. Git detects conflicts when the
is a request to merge code from one branch into another (usually

Reviewers examine:
- Code correctness and logic
- Test coverage
- Style consistency
- Potential conflicts with existing code
When Git flags a conflict, you see markers in the file:

<<<<<<< HEAD
// Your branch's changes
=======
// main branch's changes
>>>>>>> branch-name
in your editor (VS Code, IntelliJ, etc.)
- Keep only your branch's changes - Keep only

's changes - Combine both into a new change
3.
When Conflicts Happen After PR Creation
If your PR shows conflicts with
Option A: Fix Inside Your PR Branch

git checkout feat/user-auth
git pull origin main
# Resolve conflicts in editor
git add .
git commit -m "Resolve conflicts with main"
git push

git checkout feat/user-auth
git checkout -b feat/user-auth-merge-fix
git merge main
# Resolve conflicts
git add .
git commit -m "Resolved conflicts with main"
git push origin feat/user-auth-merge-fix
Then create a new PR from the helper branch.
Fix conflicts directly in your branch
AI tools can help detect if truly critical
: Don't merge without review—even AI-assisted resolution needs human code review.
AI tools now simplify conflict resolution:

:
- Quick conflict identification with explanations
- Automatic resolution suggestions via machine learning
- Contextual analysis to understand developer intent

:
- Simple conflicts: AI handles very well
- Complex conflicts: Provides suggestions, but

In a team of 10 developers at a mid-sized company:
-
: 95%PRs merge successfully within 24 hours after review
: Add comment explaining why you chose specific resolution
: Understands code structure, avoids false positives
(5–20): Helper branches + AI suggestions reduce bottleneck
(>20): Need stricter file ownership, automated conflict detection
: Enforce PR size limits (<400 lines), use AI tools, designate file owners.
VS Code (built-in conflict editor), IntelliJ (JetBrains AI)
GitHub web UI for competing line changes
Custom Git merge drivers, Semantic Merge
Rewrites history, not for shared branches

:
- Resolving with incorrect code → security vulnerabilities
- Skipping review after AI resolution → hidden bugs
- Merging conflicting config changes → deployment failures

perform code review after AI-assisted resolution
- Run tests post-resolution
- Use CI/CD pipelines to catch integration issues
- Require 2+ reviewer approvals for critical files
The following walkthrough covers the complete conflict resolution cycle from detecting conflicts to final push, with the exact git commands at each step.

# Step 1: Start your feature branch
git checkout -b feature/add-login-page

# ... do your work, make commits ...
git add src/login.js src/auth.css
git commit -m "Add login page with OAuth support"

# Step 2: Someone else pushed changes to main while you were working.
# Bring main's changes into your branch (rebase recommended for cleaner history).
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main
# --- or use merge ---
# git merge origin/main

# Step 3: Git stops at conflicted files
# CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in src/auth.js
# Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.

# Step 4: List all conflicted files
git status
# Both modified: src/auth.js

# Step 5: Open the file — conflict looks like:
cat src/auth.js
# <<<<<<< HEAD (your branch changes)
# function login(user) { return oauthLogin(user); }
# =======
# function login(user) { return localAuth(user); }
# >>>>>>> origin/main

# Step 6: Edit the file to the correct merged version,
# then remove ALL conflict markers (<<<, ===, >>>)
# Result after your edit:
# function login(user) { return oauthLogin(user) || localAuth(user); }

# Step 7: Mark as resolved
git add src/auth.js

# Step 8: Continue the rebase
git rebase --continue
# (or 'git commit' if you used merge instead of rebase)

# Step 9: Verify the build passes
npm test

# Step 10: Push
git push origin feature/add-login-page --force-with-lease  # needed after rebase
# (plain 'git push' after merge — no force needed)

When resolving a conflict in an IDE such as VS Code, the three-panel view (yours | merged result | theirs) lets you accept individual hunks without editing markers manually. This is faster for complex conflicts with many changed lines.

attrs.alt: Git conflict resolution workflow showing main branch and feature branch diverging, conflict zone, and resolution steps

content.text: Example 1 — Wrong: leaving conflict markers in the file

# BAD — always picking your version loses a bug fix from main
def process_order(order):
    # Your version (HEAD): added discount logic
    total = order.total * 0.9  # 10% discount
    return total

# main version had: total = order.total - order.discount_amount  ← bug fix
# By always picking HEAD you silently lost the bug fix.

# GOOD — apply discount on top of the corrected subtraction
def process_order(order):
    subtotal = order.total - order.discount_amount  # bug fix from main
    return subtotal * 0.9                           # feature from your branch

When git merges two branches, it does not simply compare the two branch tips. Instead it finds the common ancestor commit (the merge base) and performs a three-way merge: comparing base → ours and base → theirs separately.

A conflict is declared only when both sides changed the same region of the file relative to the base. If only one side changed a line, git applies that change automatically without a conflict marker. This is why a true conflict is rarer than it might seem — it requires genuinely overlapping edits.

The following Python code models the key decision git makes: identifying which base-file line indices were changed by both sides. Understanding this helps you predict when conflicts will arise before running git merge.

# Simulating git's three-way merge logic in Python
# This is a simplified model of what git merge-file does internally.

def three_way_merge(base: list[str], ours: list[str], theirs: list[str]) -> tuple[list[str], list[str]]:
    """
    Identify conflict regions using the three-way merge algorithm.
    Returns (merged_lines, conflict_regions).
    A conflict occurs where both sides changed the same region from base.
    """
    import difflib

    # Compute hunks: what base -> ours changed
    diff_ours = list(difflib.unified_diff(base, ours, lineterm=''))
    diff_theirs = list(difflib.unified_diff(base, theirs, lineterm=''))

    # In practice, git uses the Myers diff algorithm then applies a conflict
    # resolution pass.  Here we use Python's SequenceMatcher as a stand-in.

    matcher_ours   = difflib.SequenceMatcher(None, base, ours)
    matcher_theirs = difflib.SequenceMatcher(None, base, theirs)

    changed_ours   = {i for tag, i1, i2, _, _ in matcher_ours.get_opcodes()
                      if tag != 'equal' for i in range(i1, i2)}
    changed_theirs = {i for tag, i1, i2, _, _ in matcher_theirs.get_opcodes()
                      if tag != 'equal' for i in range(i1, i2)}

    conflict_lines = changed_ours & changed_theirs
    return conflict_lines


# Example: both sides changed line 4 (index 3)
base   = ["line1\n", "line2\n", "line3\n", "line4\n", "line5\n"]
ours   = ["line1\n", "line2\n", "line3\n", "OURS_CHANGE\n",   "line5\n"]
theirs = ["line1\n", "line2\n", "line3\n", "THEIRS_CHANGE\n", "line5\n"]

conflicts = three_way_merge(base, ours, theirs)
if conflicts:
    print(f"Conflict at base line(s): {sorted(conflicts)}")
    print("git would produce:")
    print("<<<<<<< HEAD")
    for i in conflicts:
        print(ours[i], end='')
    print("=======")
    for i in conflicts:
        print(theirs[i], end='')
    print(">>>>>>> feature-branch")
else:
    print("No conflicts — merge is automatic")
Automating Resolution with Custom Merge Drivers

Git allows you to register a custom merge driver for specific file patterns in .gitattributes. This is useful for structured files — version files, lock files, or generated configuration — where the correct resolution is deterministic.
every project that auto-generates files (package-lock.json, VERSION, *.generated.ts) benefits from a driver that resolves those mechanically.

git passes three temp-file paths (%O base, %A ours, %B theirs) to the driver script; the driver writes the resolved result into %A and exits 0 for success or non-zero to leave the conflict for a human.
set recursive = binary to tell git not to run the driver recursively during rerere.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# custom-merge-driver.py
# Register as a git merge driver in .gitconfig / .gitattributes to handle
# specific file types automatically.
#
# .gitattributes:
#   *.version merge=version-bump
#
# .git/config (or ~/.gitconfig):
#   [merge "version-bump"]
#       name = Version-bump merge driver
#       driver = python3 custom-merge-driver.py %O %A %B
#       recursive = binary

import sys
import re
import pathlib

BASE_FILE, OURS_FILE, THEIRS_FILE = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2], sys.argv[3]

def read_version(path: str) -> tuple[int, int, int]:
    """Parse semver MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH from a VERSION file."""
    text = pathlib.Path(path).read_text().strip()
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)\.(\d+)\.(\d+)', text)
    if not match:
        raise ValueError(f"No semver found in {path!r}")
    return tuple(int(x) for x in match.groups())

try:
    base  = read_version(BASE_FILE)
    ours  = read_version(OURS_FILE)
    theirs = read_version(THEIRS_FILE)

    # Strategy: take the maximum of each component
    resolved = tuple(max(a, b) for a, b in zip(ours, theirs))
    pathlib.Path(OURS_FILE).write_text(".".join(map(str, resolved)) + "\n")

    if resolved == ours == theirs:
        print(f"[driver] versions identical: {resolved}")
        sys.exit(0)
    elif ours == theirs:
        print(f"[driver] both sides bumped to same version: {resolved}")
        sys.exit(0)
    else:
        print(f"[driver] resolved version {base} -> {ours} vs {theirs} => {resolved}")
        sys.exit(0)
except Exception as e:
    print(f"[driver] error: {e} — falling back to git default", file=sys.stderr)
    sys.exit(1)

A common mistake after resolving a conflict is accidentally committing a file that still contains a conflict marker — either because the developer added the file without reviewing it or because the editor auto-saved it. A pre-commit hook that scans staged files for conflict markers stops this before it reaches the repository.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# .git/hooks/pre-commit — abort commit if conflict markers remain in any file.
# Install: cp this file .git/hooks/pre-commit && chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit

CONFLICT_PATTERN='<<<<<<< |======= |>>>>>>> '

# Check staged files only
staged=$(git diff --cached --name-only --diff-filter=ACM)

found=0
while IFS= read -r file; do
    if grep -qP "$CONFLICT_PATTERN" "$file" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "ERROR: conflict markers found in $file" >&2
        grep -nP "$CONFLICT_PATTERN" "$file" >&2
        found=1
    fi
done <<< "$staged"

if [ "$found" -eq 1 ]; then
    echo "" >&2
    echo "Resolve all conflicts before committing." >&2
    exit 1
fi
exit 0
Add this hook to every developer's environment via a shared hooks directory checked into the repository, or use a tool like pre-commit (pre-commit.com) to install and maintain it declaratively.
git config core.hooksPath .githooks lets you check hooks into source control and have every team member use them automatically.
add `- repo: local` with a `check-merge-conflict` hook to .pre-commit-config.yaml; it catches all conflict patterns across any file type.
add grep -rP '<<<<<<< |>>>>>>> ' . as a CI check step as a last-resort fallback even if local hooks are skipped.
Merge conflicts occur when two branches change the same lines differently — Git marks them with
catch conflicts and quality issues before they reach main — never skip review after resolving a conflict.
reduce conflict size; small focused PRs reduce conflict probability.
produces linear history but rewrites commit hashes — only safe on personal branches.
AI tools (JetBrains AI, CodeGPT) speed up resolution by providing context-aware suggestions, but
Always run tests after resolving conflicts — even a correctly resolved file can introduce logical inconsistencies between modules.
is the most common mistake — configure your CI to catch them with a lint rule.
